Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study every evening after work, but it rarely happened that way. I usually ended up cramming when I remembered something was due or when I got a notification. With seven online courses, I was constantly jumping between different platforms and materials, never really settling into one thing deeply. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to find diagrams or videos, but organizing when to actually watch them? That was another story.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than any real schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd have three things due and I'd be up until 2am trying to catch up. Working with limited resources means I sometimes had to wait until I could get to a place with reliable wifi or borrow a laptop. The inconsistency was exhausting, but I couldn't seem to break out of it no matter how much I wanted to.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in—seeing an email reminder about a deadline or realizing I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes my peer support group would message me asking if I'd seen the new material, and that would prompt me to check. Other times I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which honestly sometimes made me feel worse because I'd see how behind I was. It was rarely a proactive thing, more reactive.
Q4: For maybe a few weeks in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS every morning with my coffee. My peer support buddy and I had this accountability thing going where we'd message each other after logging in. But then life got complicated—extra shifts at work, some family stuff—and that routine just fell apart. Once I lost the habit, it was really hard to rebuild it, especially with my low resilience when things get tough.
Q5: The video content was definitely most useful for me since I learn visually. I could pause, rewind, and actually see concepts being explained rather than just reading walls of text. The discussion forums were also helpful when people posted questions I had but was too anxious to ask myself. Seeing my peers break down concepts in their own words sometimes clicked better than the official materials.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials—long PDFs just made my eyes glaze over. The practice quizzes I kept telling myself I'd do 'later' but later never came. I also avoided the live sessions because my schedule was unpredictable and honestly, the idea of being called on gave me anxiety. If something wasn't immediately necessary for an assignment, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my list indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment I needed to submit. Visual stuff like infographics or video walkthroughs felt valuable because I could absorb them quickly. If something took too long to load or required too many steps, I'd lose patience fast. I also valued anything my peer group recommended because I trusted their judgment on what was actually important.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're juggling seven courses and barely keeping up with required work, optional stuff feels almost insulting—like, who has time for that? Required activities had this weight to them, this pressure, while optional ones felt like they existed in some parallel universe where students have unlimited time and energy. I know they were probably helpful, but I couldn't justify spending my limited study hours on them.
Q9: Oh absolutely. I'd screenshot slides and diagrams to study on my phone during my commute or work breaks. I downloaded videos when I had good wifi and watched them offline later. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations of concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. My notes app is full of stuff I typed out while studying that would never show up in any LMS log.
Q10: There were so many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd have a downloaded PDF open for hours while I worked through problems on paper. I'd discuss course concepts with my peer support group over WhatsApp, working through ideas together. Sometimes I'd watch a course video once, then spend the next two hours drawing out diagrams and making visual notes—the LMS only saw that one video view, not all the processing that happened after.
Q11: Yeah, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it or screenshot it, and then work with it offline for days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could. I'd watch a lecture video once on campus, then review my notes from it multiple times at home. The LMS probably thought I was barely engaging when really I was just being strategic about my limited connectivity.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. I was working extra hours because I needed the money, and the stress was building up. My anxiety would spike every time I thought about how behind I was, which made me avoid the course materials even more—it was this terrible cycle. When my resilience is already low, hitting a wall like that makes it feel impossible to climb back over. The peer support helped emotionally but couldn't give me more hours in the day.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was huge—I'd log into the LMS with good intentions and then just stare at the screen, too tired to actually absorb anything. When I was confused by material, I'd sometimes just close the tab rather than push through because I didn't have the mental energy to figure it out. My work schedule competing with assignment deadlines meant I was always making impossible choices about what to prioritize. The LMS became something I dreaded opening because it just reminded me of everything I was failing at.
Q14: All the time. I'd have the LMS open in a tab while I was really scrolling my phone or zoning out. Sometimes I'd click through videos at 2x speed just to mark them as 'watched' without really taking anything in. There were nights I'd stare at reading materials for an hour and realize I hadn't absorbed a single paragraph. My mind was elsewhere—worrying about money, about failing, about everything except the actual content in front of me.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams constantly, even though I knew my exam scores were hurting my grade. The anxiety around them was so intense that avoidance felt like self-protection, even though it was self-sabotage. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, or after my next shift, or when I felt less stressed—but that day never came. It's frustrating because I could do well on assignments when I focused, but the exam pressure just paralyzed me.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be on the LMS constantly, trying to cram everything in. My login frequency probably spiked dramatically in the 48 hours before anything was due. It's not a healthy pattern, but it was the only structure that actually forced me to act. The problem is cramming doesn't lead to real learning or good exam performance.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me for maybe a day or two. My assignment scores were actually pretty good, which was confusing because I was still failing overall. But feedback on exams was demoralizing—seeing low scores made me want to disengage more, not less. The peer support intervention helped because getting encouragement from actual people felt different than just seeing a grade. Human feedback mattered more than system feedback.
Q18: Having seven online courses meant I was constantly context-switching, which made deep engagement with any single course really hard. The course structure assumed students had more time and focus than I actually had. When modules were released weekly, I'd fall behind and then face this overwhelming backlog. A more flexible structure might have helped, but honestly, I'm not sure any structure could have fixed my situation this semester.
Q19: The visual elements of the course helped me a lot—when there were good diagrams or video explanations, I could actually learn. But the sheer volume of content made engagement harder. Having discussion forums was good in theory, but they moved so fast I felt lost if I missed a few days. The attendance requirements were basically impossible with my work schedule, which made me feel like I was already failing from the start. That 10% attendance rate wasn't because I didn't care—I literally couldn't be there.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I'd be clicking around the LMS, watching videos, reading discussions, genuinely trying to understand things—but then I'd freeze when it came to actually submitting work. Sometimes the gap between understanding something and producing something feels enormous. Or maybe they're engaging with content but struggling with the specific format assignments require. High activity doesn't mean high confidence or high output.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of me, but I can see how it happens. Some people are just efficient—they know exactly what they need, they grab it, and they're done. They might be studying from textbooks or external resources that work better for them. Or they're the type who can read something once and retain it. I wish I could do that. My brain needs more repetition and more visual reinforcement, which means more time in the system but not necessarily better results.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they can't show understanding or effort or struggle. They can't see that I watched a video three times because I was confused, not because I was engaged. They can't capture the studying I did offline or the conversations with peers that helped concepts click. They definitely can't show the anxiety that made me avoid logging in even when I wanted to learn.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just going through the motions but actually thinking about the material, questioning it, trying to apply it. For me, engagement also means feeling like you're part of a community—like with my peer support group, when we'd discuss things together, that felt like real engagement. It's hard to be engaged when you're exhausted, anxious, and stretched too thin, even if you genuinely want to be.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually wanted to succeed or how hard I was fighting against my circumstances. It can't show the panic attacks before exams or the guilt I felt every time I saw my grades dropping. It doesn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from giving up entirely, or the offline studying that happened in stolen moments during work breaks. It definitely can't show that my failure wasn't from lack of caring—it was from having too much on my plate and not enough resources to manage it all.
